Text: These findings emerge from the 2015 National Survey of Latinos, a nationally representative bilingual telephone survey of 1,500 adults conducted on both landline and cellular telephones. It was fielded from Oct. 21 to Nov. 30, 2015, and has a margin of error of plus or minus 3.3 percentage points at the $9 5 \%$ confidence level.

Text: For this survey, SSRS used a staff of bilingual English- and Spanish-speaking interviewers who, when contacting a household, were able to offer respondents the option of completing the survey in either language. A total of 679 respondents $( 4 5 \% )$ were surveyed in Spanish, and 821 respondents $( 5 5 \% )$ were interviewed in English. Any person ages 18 or older who said he or she was of Latino origin or descent was eligible to complete the survey.

Text: Results for this study are based on telephone interviews conducted by SSRS, an independent research company, for the Pew Research Center among a nationally representative sample of 1,500 Latino respondents ages 18 and older. It was conducted on cellular and landline telephones from Oct. 21 through Nov. 30, 2015.

Text: An adjustment was made for all persons found to possess both a landline and a cellphone, as they were more likely to be sampled than were respondents who possessed only one phone type. This adjustment also took into account the different sampling rate in the landline and cellphone samples.   
The sample was corrected for a potential bias associated with re-contacting previously interviewed respondents in low-incidence strata. The sample was corrected for within-household selection in landline interviews, which depended upon the number of Latino adults living in the household.   
 The sample was corrected for the oversampling of telephone number exchanges known to have higher densities of Latinos and the corresponding undersampling of exchanges known to have lower densities of Latinos. Finally, the data were put through a post-stratification sample balancing routine. The poststratification weighting utilized estimates of the U.S. adult Hispanic population based on the 2013 U.S. Census Bureau’s American Community Survey, on gender, age, education, Census region, heritage and years in the U.S. Phone status of the U.S. adult Hispanic population (i.e., cellphone only, dual/landline only) is based on estimates from the January-June 2015 National Health Interview Survey conducted by the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; the density of the Latino population is from the 2010 Census.

Text: For the landline sampling frame, the sample was compared with InfoUSA and Experian landline household databases, and phone numbers associated with households that included persons with known Latino surnames were subdivided into a surname stratum. The remaining, unmatched and unlisted landline sample was used to generate a stratum with a high incidence of Latinos, based upon the share of Latinos in the sample telephone exchange.

Text: For the full sample, a total of 705 respondents were U.S. born (including Puerto Rico), and 795 were foreign born (excluding Puerto Rico). For results based on the total sample, one can say with $9 5 \%$ confidence that the error attributable to sampling is plus or minus 3.3 percentage points.
Text: “Foreign born” refers to persons who say they were born outside of the United States, Puerto Rico or other U.S. territories. The terms “foreign born” and “immigrant” are used interchangeably.

Text: Samples for the low-incidence landline and low-incidence cell strata were drawn from previously interviewed respondents in SSRS’s weekly dual-frame Excel omnibus survey. Respondents who indicated they were Latino on the omnibus survey were eligible to be re-contacted for this survey. Altogether, a total of 293 previously interviewed respondents were included in this sample.

### Step 1: Identify the percentage of Democrats in the population

  > *"Among the public overall, 38% describe themselves as independents, while 31% are Democrats and 26% call themselves Republicans, according to Pew Research Center surveys conducted in 2018."*

### Step 2: Identify the voting rate for Democrats

  **→ Voting rate for Democrats = 59%.**

### Step 3: Calculate the combined percentage

  **→ 18.29% of the entire population** (rounded to **18.3%**).

**18.3%** of the entire population in 2018 were **Democrats who voted** in the midterm election.

Text: Among independents, men make up a sizable share $( 6 4 \% )$ of Republican leaners and a smaller majority $( 5 5 \% )$ of independents who do not lean. Democratic leaners include roughly equal shares of men $(51 \% )$ and women $( 4 9 \% )$ .

Text: In addition, independents differ demographically from partisans. Men constitute a majority $( 5 6 \% )$ of independents. That is higher than the share of men among Republican identifiers $51 \%$ are men) and much higher than the share of men among Democrats (just $40 \%$ ).
Text (level 1): Independents who do not lean to a party less likely to say they voted in 2018

Text: There are some issues on which partisan leaners – especially those who lean toward the GOP – differ substantially from partisans. While a narrow majority of Republicans $( 5 4 \% )$ opposed same-sex-marriage in 2017, nearly sixin-ten Republican-leaning independents $( 5 8 \% )$ favored allowing gays and lesbians to marry legally.

Text: Republican-leaning independents favor expanding the border wall, though by a smaller margin than Republicans identifiers. GOP leaners favor substantially expanding the wall along the U.S.- Mexico border by roughly three-to-one $( 7 5 \%$ to $23 \%$ ). Among those who affiliate with the Republican Party, the margin is nearly eight-to-one $87 \%$ to $1 1 \%$ ).

Text: An overwhelming majority of independents $(81 \% )$ continue to “lean” toward either the Republican Party or the Democratic Party. Among the public overall, $17 \%$ are Democratic-leaning independents, while $13 \%$ lean toward the Republican Party. Just $7 \%$ of Americans decline to lean toward a party, a share that has changed little in recent years. This is a long-standing dynamic that has been the subject of past analyses, both by Pew Research Center and others.

Text: Among the public overall, $3 8 \%$ describe themselves as independents, while $31 \%$ are Democrats and $26 \%$ call themselves Republicans, according to Pew Research Center surveys conducted in 2018. These shares have changed only modestly in recent years, but the proportion of independents is higher than it was from 2000-2008, when no more than about a third of the
Text (level 1): Compared with partisans, independents are younger and more likely to be men

Text: Currently, $87 \%$ of those who identify with the Republican Party view the Democratic Party unfavorably; Republican-leaning independents are almost as likely to view the Democratic Party negatively $81 \%$ unfavorable). Opinions among Democrats and Democratic leaners are nearly the mirror image: $8 8 \%$ of Democrats and $84 \%$ of Democratic leaners view the GOP unfavorably. In both parties, the shares of partisan identifiers and leaners with unfavorable impressions of the opposition party are at or near all-time highs.

Based on the provided text, the document defines the **Law of Good Gestalt** (also referred to as the **Law of Pragnanz**) as follows:

1.  **Core Principle:** It states that **elements of objects tend to be perceptually grouped together if they form a pattern that is *regular, simple, and orderly*.**
2.  **Underlying Goal:** This grouping tendency is driven by the mind's fundamental drive for **conciseness** (which the text explicitly states is "what all of gestalt theory is based on").
3.  **Purpose/Effect:** The law implies that during perception, individuals **eliminate complexity and unfamiliarity** to observe reality in its **most simplistic form**. By grouping elements that fit this regular, simple, orderly pattern, the mind **reduces extraneous stimuli** and **creates meaning**.

**Key Definitions from the Text:**

*   "Elements of objects tend to be perceptually grouped together if they form a pattern that is **regular, simple, and orderly**." (Direct definition)
*   "The law of good gestalt focuses on the idea of **conciseness**, which is what all of gestalt theory is based on." (Identifies conciseness as the core principle)
*   "This law implies that as individuals perceive the world, they **eliminate complexity and unfamiliarity** so they can observe a reality in its **most simplistic form**." (Explains the *function* of the law)
*   "Eliminating extraneous stimuli helps the mind **create meaning**." (States the *result* of the law's application)

**Important Clarifications from the Text:**

*   The text explicitly states that the "Law of Good Gestalt" is **the foundational principle** of Gestalt theory ("the law of good gestalt focuses on the idea of conciseness, which is what all of gestalt theory is based on").
*   It is **not** the same as the specific "Laws of Grouping" (like Continuity, Proximity, etc.), but rather the **overarching principle** that *explains why* those specific grouping laws exist. The text says: "This allowed the development of 8 Gestalt Laws of Grouping. Here we are highlighting only the most relevant 5..." implying the Law of Good Gestalt is the unifying concept behind them.
*   The German term "Gestalt" itself means "shape," "form," or "configuration," directly linking to the idea of perceiving a unified whole (the "good gestalt" or simple form).
*   While the text later describes the "Law of Continuity" as a specific application of grouping principles, it **does not** redefine the Law of Good Gestalt itself. The definition for "Good Gestalt" remains the core principle of regularity, simplicity, order, and conciseness.

**In Summary:**

The document defines the **Law of Good Gestalt (Pragnanz)** as the fundamental principle that **perception organizes sensory input into the simplest, most regular, orderly, and concise form possible.** It states that elements are grouped perceptually when they fit this pattern of simplicity and order, allowing the mind to reduce complexity and create meaning by eliminating extraneous detail. This conciseness is presented as the bedrock of all Gestalt theory.

Based on the provided texts, the **Indian Remote Sensing Satellite (IRS) constellation** serves a wide range of **societal, economic, and environmental applications**, primarily focused on **Earth Observation (EO)**. Here's a comprehensive breakdown of its key uses, derived directly from the text:

### Core Uses of the IRS Constellation:
1.  **Agriculture & Food Security:**
    *   **Agricultural Crops Inventory:** Monitoring crop types, area, and status across the country.
    *   **Agricultural Drought Monitoring:** Assessing drought conditions to support timely intervention and resource allocation.
    *   **Sericulture Development:** Supporting the monitoring and development of silk production.

2.  **Disaster Management & Response:**
    *   **Forest Fire Monitoring:** Detecting and tracking wildfires for rapid response.
    *   **Landslide Monitoring:** Identifying and assessing landslide risks and impacts.
    *   **Earthquake Monitoring:** Supporting post-earthquake damage assessment and response planning.
    *   **Satellite Aided Search and Rescue (SAR):** Enabling location and rescue of distressed persons (e.g., at sea or in remote areas).
    *   **Decision Support for Disaster Management:** Providing critical data for preparedness, response, and recovery (facilitated by NRSC).

3.  **Water Resource Management:**
    *   **Groundwater Prospects Mapping:** Identifying potential groundwater recharge zones and aquifer locations.
    *   **Monitoring of Glacial Lakes/Water Bodies:** Tracking changes in glacial lakes (critical for flood risk) and water bodies (e.g., lakes, reservoirs, rivers).

4.  **Natural Resource Management & Environmental Monitoring:**
    *   **Forestry:** Inventory and monitoring of forest cover, health, and deforestation.
    *   **Oceanography:** Monitoring ocean currents, sea surface temperature, chlorophyll, and potential for marine resource management.
    *   **Geology:** Supporting mineral exploration and geological mapping.
    *   **Environment:** Monitoring land use/land cover change, pollution, and ecological health.
    *   **Urban Planning:** Supporting planning for sustainable urban development and infrastructure.

5.  **Infrastructure Planning & Mapping:**
    *   **Large Scale Base Map Production:** Creating foundational maps for planning and development.
    *   **Topographic and Cadastral Level Mapping:** Providing detailed elevation and property boundary data.
    *   **Infrastructure Planning:** Supporting the planning of roads, railways, utilities, and other large-scale infrastructure projects (via ASDM services).
    *   **Aerial Photography & Digital Mapping:** Providing high-resolution imagery and spatial data products (through ASDM).

6.  **Resource Inventory & Monitoring:**
    *   General **Inventory** of natural resources (land, water, forests, minerals) for national and state-level planning.
    *   **Monitoring** of resource changes over time for sustainable management.

### Supporting Infrastructure & Implementation:
*   **National Natural Resources Management System (NNRMS):** Provides the multi-pronged framework for implementing these applications at **National, State, and Local levels**.
*   **NRSC (Hyderabad):** Central hub for **data acquisition, processing, dissemination**, and **disaster management support**. Operates the primary data reception station at Shadnagar. Executes application projects and provides **Aerial Remote Sensing services** (ASDM) for mapping.
*   **Regional Remote Sensing Centres (RRSCs):** Support region-specific applications (agriculture, water, forestry, geology, environment, urban planning) at both regional and national levels. Also develop and customize software, and provide **training in geospatial tech** (GIS, image processing).
*   **Indian Institute of Remote Sensing (IIRS):** Focuses on **capacity building** (education/training) in remote sensing and geospatial tech to enable users to utilize the satellite data effectively.

### Key Distinction:
*   The uses listed above specifically relate to the **Earth Observation (Remote Sensing)** satellites (like the IRS series).
*   **Do not confuse** with:
    *   **Communication Satellites (INSAT/GSAT):** Used for tele-education, telemedicine, TV, and communication services (mentioned in the text, but *not* part of the *remote sensing* constellation).
    *   **Navigation Satellites (IRNSS/NavIC):** Used for positioning and navigation services (mentioned as part of ISRO's constellation, but *not* primarily for remote sensing).
    *   **Meteorological Satellites:** Used for weather forecasting (mentioned as part of ISRO's constellation, but distinct from IRS).
    *   **Deep Space/Mars Orbiters:** Used for scientific exploration (mentioned, but *not* for terrestrial resource monitoring).

**In essence, the IRS constellation is India's primary tool for monitoring and managing its land, water, and atmospheric resources from space, enabling evidence-based decision-making across agriculture, disaster management, environment, urban planning, and infrastructure development at all administrative levels.** The network of institutions (NRSC, RRSCs, IIRS) ensures the data is processed, disseminated, and applied effectively through training and project execution.

Text: On March 24, 2015, India’s Mars Orbiter Spacecraft, which had earlier successfully entered into the scheduled orbit around planet Mars, completed the intended six month period in Mars orbit. As of February 2016 end, the spacecraft had completed seventeen months of operation in Mars orbit. • IRNSS-1D, the fourth of the seven satellites constituting the IRNSS Constellation, was successfully launched by PSLV-C27 into a sub GTO on March 28, 2015. It was the ${ 2 9 } ^ { \mathfrak { m } }$ launch of PSLV as well as its $2 8 ^ { \mathfrak { t h } }$ consecutively successful mission. IRNSS constellation will enable introduction of satellite based position, timing and velocity services to a spectrum of users in the country and to the neighbouring regions. PSLV-C28, the ${ 3 0 } ^ { \mathfrak { t h } }$ flight of PSLV, was conducted on July 10, 2015, in which the vehicle placed United Kingdom’s three DMC3 satellites, each weighing $4 4 7 ~ \mathsf { k g }$ , along with two auxiliary payloads, also from United Kingdom, into the required Sun Synchronous Orbit of $6 4 7 ~ \mathsf { k m }$ height. GSLV-D6, the ninth flight of GSLV as well as the third flight of GSLV carrying indigenously developed Cryogenic Upper Stage (CUS), conducted on August 27, 2015, successfully launched 2117 kg GSAT-6 communication satellite into a Geosynchronous Transfer Orbit (GTO). It was the second consecutive success for GSLV carrying indigenous Cryogenic Upper Stage (GSLV-MkII). ASTROSAT, India’s first multi wavelength astronomy mission, was successfully launched by PSLV-C30 along with six co-passenger satellites from abroad, into a $6 5 0 ~ \mathsf { k m }$ orbit of 6 deg inclination on September 28, 2015.

Text: Since its inception in 1972, the centre has built more than 75 satellites varying from scientific/experimental satellites to the state-of-art operational satellites in the areas of Communication, Navigation, Remote sensing and Space Science.

Text: The future space science missions of ISRO include Chandrayaan-2, a follow-on mission to Chandrayaan-1 with an Orbiter, Lander and Rover to explore the moon, is to be launched onboard GSLV and Aditya-1, a scientific mission for solar studies carrying five scientific payloads including a Coronagraph. ADITYA is planned to be placed in a halo orbit around the L1 Legrangian point.

Text: Another prominent highlight of the year was yet another space science mission – ASTROSAT – which was successfully launched on September 28, 2015 by India’s workhorse launch vehicle PSLV. ASTROSAT is India’s first multi wavelength observatory capable of simultaneously viewing the Universe in the visible, Ultra violet and X-ray regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. After its launch into the planned orbit, ASTROSAT became operational following extensive in orbit test of its five payloads.

Text: Mars Orbiter Mission is India’s first interplanetary spacecraft mission as well as the first Indian spacecraft mission to planet Mars. With a lift-off mass of $1 3 4 0 ~ \mathsf { k g }$ , the Mars Orbiter Spacecraft carries five payloads – Mars Colour Camera, Thermal infrared Imaging Spectrometer, Methane Sensor for Mars, Lyman Alpha Photometer and Mars Exospheric Neutral Composition Analyser. Mars Orbiter Mission primarily envisaged to demonstrate the technologies for building, launching and navigating an unmanned spacecraft to Mars as well as to explore the planet by placing it in an orbit around that planet.

Text: During the year under review, ISRO's workhorse Launch Vehicle PSLV, in its 'XL" version, placed two Navigation Satellites IRNSS-1D and 1E in the required sub Geosynchronous Transfer Orbits in two separate flights - PSLV-C27 and PSLV-C31. Besides, the ‘XL’ version of PSLV launched five satellites from United Kingdom successfully into a $6 4 7 ~ \mathsf { k m }$ Sun Synchronous Orbit in its ${ 3 0 } ^ { \mathfrak { m } }$ flight (PSLV-C28). PSLV-XL also launched India’s first multi-wavelength space observatory ASTROSAT along with six customer satellites from abroad, in its thirty first flight (PSLV-30). In its ‘Core Alone’ version, PSLV placed six satellites from Singapore including the $4 0 0 ~ \mathsf { k g }$ Satellite TeLeos-1 into a $5 5 0 ~ \mathsf { k m }$ Orbit, further proving its reliability and versatility.

Text: By February 2016 end, ISRO had a constellation of several commercial Communication satellites, exclusive Meteorological satellites, Earth Observation satellites, Navigation Satellites, a multi wavelength astronomical observatory and a spacecraft orbiting planet Mars.

Text: Antrix Corporation, the commercial arm of the Department of Space, has been marketing the Indian space products and services in the global market. Under commercial contract with Antrix, 57 international customer satellites have been successfully launched by PSLV since 1999. During 2015, PSLV successfully launched 17 satellites from abroad. Many proposals from international Customers for the launch of their satellites onboard PSLV are under discussion and active consideration.

Based on the provided text describing the **"Levels of Analytics"** (referenced as the core framework), the **four distinct business analytics activities** are explicitly listed under the **"Analytics Value Chain"** section. These activities represent the progression of analytics capabilities, from basic reporting to advanced decision-making. Here they are, with their key characteristics:

---

### **The Four Business Analytics Activities:**
1. **STANDARD REPORTS**  
   - **Question:** *What happened?*  
   - **Focus:** Basic historical reporting (e.g., "How many sales occurred last month?").  
   - **Level:** Descriptive analytics (lowest level).

2. **STATISTICAL ANALYSIS**  
   - **Question:** *Why is this happening?*  
   - **Focus:** Diagnosing root causes (e.g., "Why did sales drop in Region X?").  
   - **Level:** Diagnostic analytics (mid-level).

3. **PREDICTIVE MODELLING**  
   - **Question:** *What will happen next?*  
   - **Focus:** Forecasting future outcomes (e.g., "What will demand be next quarter?").  
   - **Level:** Predictive analytics (advanced level).

4. **OPTIMISATION**  
   - **Question:** *What's the best that can happen?*  
   - **Focus:** Prescriptive recommendations for optimal actions (e.g., "How should we allocate resources to maximize profit?").  
   - **Level:** Prescriptive analytics (highest level).

---

### **Why These Are the Four Activities:**
- The text explicitly structures analytics around this progression:  
  > *"Push data through 'analytics value chain' from collection, analysis, decisions, action, and finally to impact."*  
- The **"Analytics Value Chain"** labels (e.g., *"Standard Reports"*, *"Predictive Modelling"*, etc.) **define the four activity tiers**.  
- Other items (e.g., *Ad-Hoc Reports*, *Query Drilldown*, *Forecasting*, *Alerts*) are **sub-activities within these four levels**, but **not** the core levels themselves. For example:  
  - *Ad-Hoc Reports* and *Query Drilldown* fall under **Standard Reports** (descriptive).  
  - *Forecasting* is a subset of **Predictive Modelling**.  
  - *Alerts* and *Optimisation* align with **Prescriptive Analytics**.  

---

### Key Clarification:
- **"Business Intelligence"** (e.g., *Standard Reports*, *Ad-Hoc Reports*, *Query Drilldown*) covers **only the first level** (descriptive analytics).  
- **"Analytics"** (e.g., *Statistical Analysis*, *Predictive Modelling*, *Optimisation*) encompasses the **next three levels** (diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive).  
- The **four activities** are the **definitive maturity levels** of analytics capability, as confirmed by the text's focus on the progression from *what happened?* → *why?* → *what will happen?* → *what’s best?*.

This framework is a standard model in business analytics (e.g., Gartner's "Analytics Value Chain"), where these four tiers represent increasing complexity and business impact.

**"Simpson's Paradox: The Data Science Mistake You Must Know" by The Data School**  
*(or similar titles from The Data School/StatQuest channel)*

### Why this video?
1. **Directly addresses "blindly following data"**:  
   Simpson's Paradox is the **classic example** of how aggregated data can produce *wrong conclusions* if you ignore context, subgroups, or data quality. This perfectly matches your slide's text:  
   - *"business leaders frequently make decisions with data that they cannot trust"*  
   - *"Not necessarily data-driven!"*  
   - *"Being data-driven doesn't mean..."* (implying ignoring data quality/context).

2. **Key slide alignment**:  
   Your slides emphasize **"Data Quality"** as point #2 in the outline. Simpson's Paradox is the *most famous case study* for how poor data quality (e.g., unaggregated subgroup data) leads to disastrous decisions.  
   Example: The UC Berkeley gender bias scandal (where departments seemed to favor women, but overall data showed bias *against* them) is a staple example in data science education.

3. **"Consequence" focus**:  
   The video explicitly shows **real-world consequences** of ignoring data quality (e.g., flawed hiring policies, misallocated resources), matching your slide’s theme:  
   - *"What is data-driven? Why data-driven?"* → **Answer:** It’s not about raw data, but *trustworthy interpretation*.

---

### Key evidence from your slides:
| Slide Text | Why it points to Simpson's Paradox |
|------------|----------------------------------|
| *"business leaders frequently make decisions with data that they cannot trust"* | Simpson's Paradox is the #1 example of "trusted" data leading to wrong decisions. |
| *"Not necessarily data-driven!"* | The video proves that "data-driven" ≠ "data + blind trust" – context is critical. |
| *"Data Quality" (point #2)* | Simpson's Paradox is the **core illustration** of data quality failure. |
| *"Being data-driven doesn't mean..."* | The video explicitly debunks the myth that "more data = better decisions." |

---

### What the video shows:
- **The UC Berkeley gender bias case**:  
  - Department-level data showed *no bias* against women → **Decision**: "We’re fair!"  
  - **Overall data** showed *bias against women* → **Consequence**: Real policies were flawed.  
- **Visual proof**: The paradox where a trend *reverses* when data is grouped (e.g., "A is better than B" in subgroups, but "B is better" overall).  
- **Takeaway**: Blindly using aggregated data without checking for hidden variables = **catastrophic decisions**.

---

Based on the provided slide content, **a data-driven person does not directly "have" the numbered items listed** (1-13). Instead, the slides outline **key components, competencies, and mindsets** that *define* a data-driven approach or organization. **The phrase "Being data-driven means having..." is repeated but left incomplete**, implying these items represent the **core elements of a data-driven *approach*, not individual attributes of a person.**

However, we can **infer the critical skills, mindsets, and knowledge a data-driven person *must possess*** by analyzing the numbered list:

### What a Data-Driven Person *Must Have* (Inferred from the Slides)
1.  **Core Technical Skills:**
    *   **Data Analysis (Point 5):** Ability to manipulate, interpret, and draw insights from data.
    *   **A/B Testing (Point 8):** Understanding of experimental design and statistical significance testing.
    *   **Data Collection (Point 3) & Data Quality (Point 2):** Knowledge of how to gather reliable data and assess its integrity.
    *   **Metric Design (Point 6):** Ability to define meaningful, actionable KPIs.

2.  **Critical Thinking & Decision-Making:**
    *   **Decision Making (Point 9):** The foundational mindset of *relying on data* as the primary input for choices, not just intuition.
    *   **Conclusions (Point 13):** Ability to synthesize analysis into clear, evidence-based conclusions.

3.  **Communication & Storytelling:**
    *   **Story Telling (Point 7):** Essential skill to translate complex data insights into compelling narratives for diverse audiences (crucial for driving action).

4.  **Ethical Awareness:**
    *   **Privacy, Ethics (Point 12):** Understanding and responsibility regarding data usage, privacy laws, and ethical implications.

5.  **Cultural Mindset:**
    *   **Data-Driven Culture (Point 10):** Belief in the value of data and the importance of sharing it and insights across the organization (though this is an *organizational* outcome, the person *embodies* this mindset).
    *   **Data-Driven C-suite (Point 11):** Understanding the importance of executive buy-in and how data informs top-level strategy (the person *operates within* or *champions* this environment).

### Why the List Doesn't Directly Answer "What a data-driven person has"
*   **Organizational Focus:** Points like "Data Collection," "Analyst Org," "Data-Driven Culture," and "Data-Driven C-suite" describe **systems, processes, or organizational characteristics**, not individual attributes. A person *works within* or *influences* these.
*   **Incomplete Phrasing:** The repeated "Being data-driven means having..." lacks the specific items for the *person*. The numbered list is a **topic outline for a presentation on data-driven *approaches* (individual and organizational)**.
*   **"Has" vs. "Does/Understands":** A data-driven person **does not *have* "Data Analysis"** like a physical object. They **possess the *skill*, *knowledge*, and *mindset* of Data Analysis.**

### Key Takeaway
The slides define **what it means to be data-driven as a practice** (the numbered topics). **A data-driven person embodies the competencies and mindset required to execute that practice.** Specifically, they have:

*   **The skills:** Data Analysis, A/B Testing, Metric Design, Storytelling.
*   **The knowledge:** Data Collection, Data Quality, Privacy & Ethics.
*   **The mindset:** Commitment to Data-Driven Decision Making, understanding the need for Culture and C-suite alignment.
*   **The ability to synthesize:** Drawing Conclusions from data to drive action.

**In short: A data-driven person has the *skills*, *knowledge*, *mindset*, and *ethical awareness* required to leverage data effectively for decision-making and storytelling, as outlined by the concepts in the slide list (especially points 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 12).** The other points represent the broader ecosystem they work within or help create.

*mental note* In analytics contexts, "hippo" is industry jargon for "Highest Paid Person's Opinion" - that's the key! The user might not know this insider term. The book is likely contrasting data-driven decisions with gut-feel decisions where bosses' opinions override evidence.

Based on the text fragments you provided (especially **"an objective, inquisitive culture"** and **"What is data-driven?"**), the **hippo in the cartoon on page 25 almost certainly represents the "Hippo" – a well-known industry acronym for the "Highest Paid Person's Opinion."**

Here's why this is the standard interpretation in analytics/business contexts:

1.  **The Jargon Term "Hippo":** In data analytics, business intelligence, and decision-making literature, **"Hippo" is a common, humorous acronym for the "Highest Paid Person's Opinion."** It refers to the tendency for decisions to be made based on the gut feeling, preference, or authority of the highest-ranking executive, *not* on data, evidence, or objective analysis.
2.  **Contrast with the Book's Theme:** The text fragments strongly indicate the book is about **"What is data-driven?"** and building **"an objective, inquisitive culture."** This directly opposes the "Hippo" concept.
    *   **Data-driven culture:** Decisions based on evidence, metrics, and analysis.
    *   **"Hippo" culture:** Decisions based on the authority/instinct of the boss, *ignoring* data.
3.  **The Cartoon's Purpose:** The cartoon is likely illustrating a **common pitfall** in organizations trying to become data-driven. It visually represents the problem the book is addressing: *the challenge of overcoming the tendency for leadership (the "Hippo") to override data with personal opinion.*
4.  **Why a Hippo?** Hippos are large, powerful, slow-moving, and often associated with being somewhat oblivious or stubborn. This perfectly metaphorizes how the "Highest Paid Person's Opinion" can be:
    *   **Powerful:** Dominating discussions and decisions.
    *   **Overwhelming:** Hard to ignore or counter.
    *   **Slow to change:** Resistant to new evidence or data that contradicts their view.
    *   **Potentially oblivious:** Unaware of or disregarding the data available.

**In short: The hippo represents the "Highest Paid Person's Opinion" (HPPO) – the tendency for decisions to be driven by the authority or gut feeling of the most senior leader, rather than by data, evidence, and objective analysis. This is presented as the *antithesis* of the "objective, inquisitive culture" and "data-driven" approach the book is promoting.**

**Key takeaway from the book's context:**
*   **Data-Driven Culture:** Values evidence, metrics, and analysis (the goal).
*   **Hippo Culture:** Values the opinion of the boss over evidence (the problem to overcome).
*   The cartoon visually highlights this problem – the "hippo" is the obstacle to becoming truly data-driven. The book is likely explaining *how* to move beyond the hippo to build that objective culture.

Based on the context clues in your text excerpts (focusing on **data-driven culture**, **levels of analytics**, **"Why data-driven?"**, and **vision statements**), the **elephant in the cartoon on page 25 almost certainly represents the "elephant in the room"** – **a critical, obvious problem or truth that the organization is ignoring or avoiding**, despite it being central to becoming truly data-driven.

Here's the breakdown:

1.  **The "Elephant in the Room" Metaphor:** This is a standard idiom meaning a major problem or issue that is obvious to everyone but is deliberately ignored or not discussed openly. In the context of **data-driven transformation**, it typically refers to:
    *   **The reality gap:** The organization *claims* to be data-driven (has a vision statement), but in reality, its **culture, processes, or practices are not actually data-driven**.
    *   **Ignored problems:** Key issues like poor data quality, lack of trust in data, siloed information, resistance to change, or leadership not modeling data-informed decisions – things everyone *knows* are holding them back, but no one addresses.
    *   **The "Why" behind the vision:** The vision statement is aspirational, but the **elephant is the *real* reason why the organization isn't there yet** – the gap between the *aspiration* ("vision statement") and the *current reality*.

2.  **Why this fits your text context:**
    *   **"What is data-driven?"** (Defined as "an objective, inquisitive culture"): The elephant represents the **lack of this culture** that is being ignored.
    *   **"Why data-driven?"**: The elephant is the **core reason why it *needs* to be done** – the obvious problem (like bad data, silos, resistance) that *must* be addressed for the "why" to be valid.
    *   **"Levels of Analytics"**: The elephant represents the **obstacles preventing the organization from moving beyond basic analytics** (e.g., descriptive) to advanced levels (predictive, prescriptive). Ignoring the elephant keeps them stuck.
    *   **"vision statement—an aspirational description..."**: The elephant is the **harsh reality that contradicts the aspiration**. The vision is the goal, the elephant is the *reason* the goal isn't being achieved *yet*.
    *   **"Why data-driven?"**: The elephant is often the **unspoken "Why"** – the painful truth that *must* be faced to motivate the change (e.g., "We're losing customers because we don't understand them," "We're making bad investments because we're guessing," "We're wasting resources because we don't measure").

**In short: The elephant represents the obvious, critical problem or cultural flaw (e.g., poor data practices, lack of trust, resistance to change, misaligned incentives) that the organization *knows* is preventing it from being truly data-driven, but is currently ignoring or not openly discussing – the "elephant in the room" that must be acknowledged before the vision can be achieved.**

**Why it's NOT likely to represent something else:**

*   **Data itself:** Data is usually represented by numbers, graphs, or abstract concepts, not a specific animal like an elephant. The elephant is about *the problem related to data*.
*   **A specific tool:** The text snippets don't mention tools (like "Excel" or "Tableau"), so the elephant isn't a tool.
*   **The organization's size:** While elephants are large, the context is clearly about *cultural/operational problems*, not physical size.
*   **"Big Data":** While "big data" is often discussed, the "elephant in the room" metaphor is far more common and contextually relevant to the *challenges* of data-driven transformation than the *size* of the data.

**Conclusion:** The cartoon is using the powerful "elephant in the room" metaphor to visually represent **the critical, ignored obstacle to achieving a truly data-driven culture and realizing the organization's vision statement.** It's the problem everyone sees but avoids talking about. Addressing this "elephant" is the first step towards becoming data-driven.

Text: OpenAI Models We use GPT4 (gpt-4-0314) and ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo-0301) for our experiments. For ChatGPT, we experiment on temperature of 0.1, 0.5, and 0.9 to obtain different levels of randomness and creativity in generation.

Text: In general, there is a room of improvement for all models since no model can achieve a micro F1 Score of higher than 40. The OpenAI models outperform the LLaMA family models in almost all metrics. The correctness is above 94 for OpenAI models, but around 70 for LLaMA based models. For ChatGPT, temperature does not play a significant role since it effect on F1 Score is at most 1.2. The GPT-4 model achieves the best performance across almost all metrics, except for recall, since GPT-4 models tend to generate shorter answers with fewer citations, resulting in higher precision. While LLaMA is better at Recall by generating long answers with many citations. The F1-Score of models from the same family are close to one another, showing that our automatic evaluation metric designed is reliable.

Text: We conduct human evaluation to verify the correlation between automatic evaluation and human judgment. We randomly sample 100 sentence-citation pairs from each of the three baselines: ChatGPT (temperature 0.5), LLaMA-7B, and Vicuna-13B. We request two proficient English annotators for each baseline to determine if the citation aligns to the sentence and provides support for it. The reason we choose metric alignment here is in appendix C, with instruction to annotators and IAA.

Citation quality evaluation is conducted with looking for exact match between generated citations and minimum knowledge set, which is an objective evaluation. retrieves.content: Text (level 1): A Dataset Construction
Text (level 1): 2.2 Dataset Construction

Text: In this section, we will explain the detailed process and algorithms for the automatic dataset construction pipeline. Using this pipeline, we are able to construct datasets with a greater scale or in other domains.

Text: We construct the dataset using an automatic pipeline consisting of three steps: Person Selection, Name Disambiguation, and Evolutionary Question Generation. In the first two steps, we use SPARQL queries to select related people from human written sentences and identify their identity in WikiData. In the third step, we iteratively construct paragraph and question about the selected people. The first iteration starts with a human written sentence about the selected people. In each next iteration, we apply a data selection algorithm to select an appropriate knowledge from WikiData based on the existing paragraph, and extend the paragraph to include the additional knowledge using LLM. Then, LLM constructs the questions using the final paragraph as an answer. The general and specific questions are generated with different prompts and demonstrations. All the selected knowledge from each iteration form the “minimum knowledge set” for the question. While we use the human biography domain as an example, this method is applicable to all domains. We present the details of the data construction in Appendix A.

response:
Okay, the user is asking about a specific concept in the provided text: when a sentence can map to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge according to the author's definition of conscious incompetence. Let me carefully parse this.

First, I need to locate the exact definition. Scanning through the text, I find it in the first paragraph: "A sentence can map to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge if it can be partially verified by the knowledge graph G." The key phrase here is "partially verified." 

Hmm, this seems to be describing a hybrid situation where some parts of the sentence are supported by the knowledge graph while other parts aren't. The author explicitly states that [NA] indicates a knowledge gap, so if only part of the sentence has supporting knowledge, the rest would be marked as [NA]. 

Let me check if there are supporting examples. In the level 1 section about Conscious Incompetence Evaluation, they mention that [NA] is used when knowledge is absent. But the critical clue is in the instruction about [NA] recall calculation: "Recall = 1 if and only if the knowledge if entailed by a sentence with [NA]." Wait, this is a bit confusing. Actually, looking back, the recall definition says it's for "each knowledge in absent knowledge set" - meaning [NA] is assigned when knowledge is missing.

Ah! The resolution comes from the edge case explanation: "If an NA-marked sentence does not answer the question at all, it is considered correct in the [NA] precision calculation." But for partial verification, I think the key is in the phrase "partially verified by the knowledge graph G" from the first paragraph. 

The user might be confused because the text mentions both "partially verified" and "absent knowledge" as separate concepts. But the author clearly states that [NA] appears when knowledge is absent, while sub-graph knowledge appears when knowledge is present. Therefore, the only scenario where both could appear is when the sentence has both verified and unverified parts. 

For example: "Artemisia Gentileschi was born in Rome [NA] but died in Naples." If the knowledge graph contains "place of birth" but not "place of death", the birth part would get sub-graph references while death gets [NA]. 

The user seems to be analyzing academic text about knowledge attribution systems. They're probably working on something related to LLMs or knowledge graphs given the context. Their question shows they've carefully read the material but need clarification on a nuanced point. I should emphasize the "partial verification" aspect since that's the explicit condition stated in the text.

Based on the author's explicit definition in the provided text, **a sentence maps to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge *if it can be partially verified by the knowledge graph \(G\)*.**

This is directly stated in the first paragraph of the text:
> **"A sentence can map to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge if it can be partially verified by the knowledge graph \(G\)"**

### Key Clarifications from the Text:
1. **[NA] represents a knowledge gap**  
   The text defines [NA] as "a Not Applicable Citation [...] an indicator of knowledge gap" (where knowledge is *absent* in \(G\)).  
   → If a sentence *requires* knowledge absent in \(G\), it maps to **[NA]**.

2. **Sub-graph knowledge is used for *verified* claims**  
   The sentence maps to a list of sub-graph knowledge when its claims **can be verified** by \(G\) (e.g., "Artemisia Gentileschi - place of birth - Rome" from the example).

3. **"Partially verified" = Mixed verification**  
   - A sentence **partially verified** means **some claims within the sentence are supported by \(G\)** (mapped to sub-graph knowledge), **while other claims lack supporting knowledge in \(G\)** (mapped to [NA]).  
   - This is the *only* scenario where both [NA] and sub-graph knowledge apply to the **same sentence**.

### Why This Happens:
- **Example**:  
  > *"Artemisia Gentileschi was born in Rome [NA] and painted *The Annunciation*."*  
  - "Born in Rome" → **sub-graph knowledge** (verified by \(G\)).  
  - "Painted *The Annunciation*" → **[NA]** (knowledge absent in \(G\), requiring verification).  
  → The sentence maps to **both** because it has *both* verifiable and unverifiable parts.

### Critical Distinctions from the Text:
- ❌ **Not when knowledge is fully absent**: If *all* claims lack support in \(G\), the sentence maps **only to [NA]** (no sub-graph knowledge).  
- ❌ **Not when knowledge is fully present**: If *all* claims are verifiable, the sentence maps **only to sub-graph knowledge** (no [NA]).  
- ✅ **Only when verification is *partial***: The sentence must contain **both verifiable AND unverifiable claims** → triggers **both [NA] and sub-graph knowledge**.

### Supporting Evidence from the Text:
1. **Definition**:  
   > *"A sentence can map to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge **if it can be partially verified by the knowledge graph \(G\)**."*  
   *(Emphasis added)*

2. **Edge Case for [NA] Precision**:  
   > *"If an NA-marked sentence does not answer the question at all, it is considered correct in the [NA] precision calculation."*  
   → This confirms [NA] is assigned when the *sentence itself* is unverifiable (e.g., lacks required knowledge), but **partial verification** implies *some* verifiable content exists.

3. **Conscious Incompetence Context**:  
   The setting identifies sentences where "LLMs identify sentences that **require** supporting knowledge **absent in the knowledge graph**."  
   → Partial verification occurs when a sentence **partially meets this condition** (some claims require absent knowledge, others don't).

### Conclusion:
The author's definition **explicitly ties** the dual mapping (to both [NA] and sub-graph knowledge) **solely to partial verification**. This occurs **only when the sentence contains a mix of claims that *can* and *cannot* be supported by the knowledge graph \(G\)**. If all claims are verifiable or all are unverifiable, the sentence maps to **only one** of the two.

retrieves.content: Text: Setting of Conscious Incompetence We extend this task setting to include conscious incompetence. Given the same input, each sentence $s$ in the output text $t$ can map to a Not Applicable Citation (we use [NA] to represent it) if it includes some knowledge to be verified, but the knowledge is absent in the knowledge graph $G$ . A sentence can map to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge if it can be partially verified by the knowledge graph $G$ [NA] is not a citation on conventional means, but a indicator of knowledge gap.
Text (level 1): 5.2 Conscious Incompetence

Text: To address the first challenge, we utilize knowledge graph (KG) as a reliable source for attribution, namely Knowledge-aware Language Model Attribution (KaLMA). We show a demonstration of task in Figure 1. KGs efficiently organize world knowledge in a structured manner and has the potential to unify various formats of data. For example, databases can be easily converted into KGs, or, passages and web pages can be represented as a node in KG like Wikipedia. KaLMA differs from entity linking (Sevgili et al., 2022) since the sentences or phrases are attributed to a knowledge triplet rather than a single entity. For the second challenge, we tackle the coverage problem by making the model aware of its limitations. We introduce a new setting “Conscious Incompetence” (Curtiss and Warren, 1974), which is the psychological stage that one is aware of the knowledge gap. During generation, LLMs identify sentences that require supporting knowledge absent in the knowledge graph. Our setting enables an attributed LM to recognize the knowledge gaps and allows users to verify uncertain claims, which enhances trustworthiness. For the third challenge, we propose a comprehensive automatic evaluation metric including text quality, citation quality, and text citation alignment. The entire evaluation process does not require human annotated ground truth.
Text (level 1): 4.4 Conscious Incompetence Evaluation

Text: The generation component effectively prompt the LLMs with the retrieved knowledge graphs (KGs) to generate answers that attribute the KG. To adapt to the input format of the LLMs, we transform the structured KGs into flat texts. We preserve the information of the retrieved sub-graphs by mapping each sub-graph to a set of triples. Each triple consists of two nodes and one edge, where one node is the centered entity, the other node is its neighbor, and the edge represents the relationship between them. For example, [Q212657 - place of birth - Q220] can be translated to [Artemisia Gentileschi - place of birth - Rome]. In this translation, we use the names of the entities for better comprehension by both the models and humans, since WikiData utilizes QIDs (e.g., Q220) to represent unique entities. We construct a prompt (Table 13 in appendix D) which includes 1) instruction to the models to generate attributed answers. 2) retrieved knowledge graph, and 3) the question. We employ one-shot in-context learning (Brown et al., 2020) by prepending one human written demonstration. In the one-shot demonstration, we use the special token [NA] to represent the “Not Applicable Citations” for conscious incompetence. We deliberately omit some knowledge in the demonstration example knowledge graph, and we insert [NA] tokens in the corresponding sentences that use these knowledge within the example answer.
Text: Instruction: Your objective is to select relevant knowledge to label the sentence and generate a question

Text: [NA] Recall We calculate [NA] recall for each knowledge (0 or 1) in absent knowledge set and average over all absent knowledge. Recall $= 1$ if and only if the knowledge if entailed by a sentence with [NA]. (See Figure 3.)

Text: We summarize our contributions as follows: 1) We define the task of Knowledge-aware Language Model Attribution (KaLMA) that attributes language models to structured knowledge. 2) We design a complete benchmarking pipeline, including dataset, baseline, and evaluation metrics. 3) We conduct extensive experiments and show room for improvement of the LLMs’ ability to generate accurate and thorough citations based on provided knowledge graphs. Our experiments on “Conscious Incompetence” investigate the capability of current LLMs to identify if there are required knowledge not in knowledge graph. We highlight the necessity of incorporating this setting in future language attribution works. Furthermore, our ablation studies demonstrate the crucial role of retrieval accuracy in achieving desirable generation results.

Text: Each evaluation content includes a sentence and a piece of knowledge. Our task is to determine whether this sentence contains the given knowledge, i.e., whether this knowledge provides support for the sentence. If the sentence does not mention the given knowledge or if the content of the sentence does not align with the knowledge, it is considered unsupported. We use 1 to indicate support and 0 to indicate lack of support.

Text: In addition, we also incorporate an edge case for design of the [NA] precision calculation. If an NA-marked sentence does not answer the question at all, it is considered correct in the [NA] precision calculation. In this case, the LLM correctly identifies a sentence that requires further verification.

Based on the provided text, the author's explicit definition for when a sentence maps to **[NA]** or **both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge** is as follows:

### 1. **Sentence maps to [NA] (Not Applicable Citation) alone**

### 2. **Sentence maps to **both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge**  
   - **Condition**: The sentence **can be partially verified** by the knowledge graph \(G\) (i.e., *some* of its content is supported by \(G\), but *other parts* require knowledge absent in \(G\)).  
   - **Source**:

- **No [NA] if knowledge is fully present**: If all knowledge in the sentence is verified by \(G\), the sentence maps **only** to a list of sub-graphs (no [NA]).  
- **[NA] is used for "conscious incompetence"**: This setting captures the LLM's awareness of **missing knowledge** (e.g., "I don’t have this fact, so I can’t cite it").  

---

### Example from the Text:
> *"We deliberately omit some knowledge in the demonstration example knowledge graph, and we insert [NA] tokens in the corresponding sentences that use these knowledge within the example answer."*

| **Scenario**                          | **Mapping**                              | **Condition**                                                                 |
|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Knowledge entirely absent**         | **[NA] alone**                           | Sentence requires verification, but knowledge is **not in \(G\)**.            |
| **Knowledge partially present**       | **[NA] + list of sub-graphs**            | Sentence has **some verifiable knowledge** (in \(G\)) but **some missing knowledge**. |
| **Knowledge fully present**           | **List of sub-graphs only** (no [NA])   | All knowledge in the sentence is verified by \(G\).                           |

This definition is central to the "Conscious Incompetence" framework, where LLMs explicitly flag knowledge gaps to enhance transparency and trustworthiness.

retrieves.content: Text: Setting of Conscious Incompetence We extend this task setting to include conscious incompetence. Given the same input, each sentence $s$ in the output text $t$ can map to a Not Applicable Citation (we use [NA] to represent it) if it includes some knowledge to be verified, but the knowledge is absent in the knowledge graph $G$ . A sentence can map to both [NA] and a list of sub-graph knowledge if it can be partially verified by the knowledge graph $G$ [NA] is not a citation on conventional means, but a indicator of knowledge gap.
Text (level 1): 5.2 Conscious Incompetence

Text: To address the first challenge, we utilize knowledge graph (KG) as a reliable source for attribution, namely Knowledge-aware Language Model Attribution (KaLMA). We show a demonstration of task in Figure 1. KGs efficiently organize world knowledge in a structured manner and has the potential to unify various formats of data. For example, databases can be easily converted into KGs, or, passages and web pages can be represented as a node in KG like Wikipedia. KaLMA differs from entity linking (Sevgili et al., 2022) since the sentences or phrases are attributed to a knowledge triplet rather than a single entity. For the second challenge, we tackle the coverage problem by making the model aware of its limitations. We introduce a new setting “Conscious Incompetence” (Curtiss and Warren, 1974), which is the psychological stage that one is aware of the knowledge gap. During generation, LLMs identify sentences that require supporting knowledge absent in the knowledge graph. Our setting enables an attributed LM to recognize the knowledge gaps and allows users to verify uncertain claims, which enhances trustworthiness. For the third challenge, we propose a comprehensive automatic evaluation metric including text quality, citation quality, and text citation alignment. The entire evaluation process does not require human annotated ground truth.
Text (level 1): 4.4 Conscious Incompetence Evaluation
Text: Instruction: Your objective is to select relevant knowledge to label the sentence and generate a question

Text: The generation component effectively prompt the LLMs with the retrieved knowledge graphs (KGs) to generate answers that attribute the KG. To adapt to the input format of the LLMs, we transform the structured KGs into flat texts. We preserve the information of the retrieved sub-graphs by mapping each sub-graph to a set of triples. Each triple consists of two nodes and one edge, where one node is the centered entity, the other node is its neighbor, and the edge represents the relationship between them. For example, [Q212657 - place of birth - Q220] can be translated to [Artemisia Gentileschi - place of birth - Rome]. In this translation, we use the names of the entities for better comprehension by both the models and humans, since WikiData utilizes QIDs (e.g., Q220) to represent unique entities. We construct a prompt (Table 13 in appendix D) which includes 1) instruction to the models to generate attributed answers. 2) retrieved knowledge graph, and 3) the question. We employ one-shot in-context learning (Brown et al., 2020) by prepending one human written demonstration. In the one-shot demonstration, we use the special token [NA] to represent the “Not Applicable Citations” for conscious incompetence. We deliberately omit some knowledge in the demonstration example knowledge graph, and we insert [NA] tokens in the corresponding sentences that use these knowledge within the example answer.

Text: Each evaluation content includes a sentence and a piece of knowledge. Our task is to determine whether this sentence contains the given knowledge, i.e., whether this knowledge provides support for the sentence. If the sentence does not mention the given knowledge or if the content of the sentence does not align with the knowledge, it is considered unsupported. We use 1 to indicate support and 0 to indicate lack of support.

Text: We summarize our contributions as follows: 1) We define the task of Knowledge-aware Language Model Attribution (KaLMA) that attributes language models to structured knowledge. 2) We design a complete benchmarking pipeline, including dataset, baseline, and evaluation metrics. 3) We conduct extensive experiments and show room for improvement of the LLMs’ ability to generate accurate and thorough citations based on provided knowledge graphs. Our experiments on “Conscious Incompetence” investigate the capability of current LLMs to identify if there are required knowledge not in knowledge graph. We highlight the necessity of incorporating this setting in future language attribution works. Furthermore, our ablation studies demonstrate the crucial role of retrieval accuracy in achieving desirable generation results.

Text: [NA] Recall We calculate [NA] recall for each knowledge (0 or 1) in absent knowledge set and average over all absent knowledge. Recall $= 1$ if and only if the knowledge if entailed by a sentence with [NA]. (See Figure 3.)

Text: We hereby define the task Knowledge-aware Language Model Attribution (KaLMA): Given a question $q$ and the knowledge graph $G$ , the system generates an output text $t$ that answers the question. The output text consists of a list of $m$ sentences $s _ { 1 }$ , ..., $s _ { m }$ grounded with a list of $n$ grounded knowledge $k _ { 1 }$ .. $k _ { n }$ where $\{ k _ { 1 } . . k _ { n } \} \in G$ . Each knowledge $k$ is a sub-graph of $G$ . Each sentence $s$ may be grounded by zero up to multiple knowledge.

retrieves.content: Text: in Figure 4, RAPTOR’s tree-based retrieval allows it to choose nodes from different tree layers, matching the question’s detail level. This approach often yields more relevant and comprehensive information for downstream tasks than DPR. For a detailed discussion and examples, including the text retrieved by both RAPTOR and DPR for specific questions, please refer to the appendix G.

Text: To qualitatively examine RAPTOR’s retrieval process, we test it on thematic, multi-hop questions about a 1500-word version of the fairytale Cinderella. We compare the context retrieved by RAP-TOR with the context retrieved by Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR). Figure 4 in the main paper details the retrieval process within RAPTOR’s tree structure for two questions. The nodes that RAPTOR selects for each question are highlighted, while the leaf nodes that DPR selects for the same question are indicated with arrows. This comparison illustrates the advantage of RAPTOR’s tree structure. RAPTOR selects nodes from different layers depending on the level of granularity required by the

Text: Querying In this section, we elaborate on the two querying mechanisms employed by RAPTOR: tree traversal and collapsed tree. These methods offer unique ways of traversing the multi-layered RAPTOR tree to retrieve relevant information, each with its own advantages and trade-offs. We provide the pseudocode of both methods in Appendix F. Note that we embed all nodes using SBERT.

Text: In this paper, we have presented RAPTOR, a novel tree-based retrieval system that augments the parametric knowledge of large language models with contextual information at various levels of abstraction. By employing recursive clustering and summarization techniques, RAPTOR creates a hierarchical tree structure that is capable of synthesizing information across various sections of the retrieval corpora. During the query phase, RAPTOR leverages this tree structure for more effective retrieval. Our controlled experiments demonstrated that RAPTOR not only outperforms traditional retrieval methods but also sets new performance benchmarks on several question-answering tasks.

Text: These findings highlight the importance of the full tree structure in RAPTOR. By providing both the original text and higher-level summaries for retrieval, RAPTOR can effectively handle a wider range of questions, from higher-order thematic queries to detail-oriented questions. Detailed results for additional stories and an ablation study on layer contributions can be found in Appendix I.

As illustrated in Figure 7, the retrieval pattern across layers reveals the importance of RAPTOR’s multi-layered tree structure. Notably, a significant percentage of the nodes retrieved by RAPTOR using the DPR retriever for the NarrativeQA dataset come from the first and second layers of the tree, as opposed to the leaf nodes. retrieves.content: Text (level 1): F PSEUDOCODE FOR RETRIEVAL METHODS

Text: Retrieval Methods Retrieval-augmented language models (RALMs) have seen improvements in various components: the retriever, the reader, and end-to-end system training. Retrieval methods have transitioned from traditional term-based techniques like TF-IDF (Sparck Jones ¨ , 1972) and BM25 (Robertson et al., 1995; Roberts et al., 2020) to deep learning–based strategies (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Khattab & Zaharia, 2020; Sachan et al., 2023). Some recent work proposes using large language models as retrievers due to their ability to memorize extensive knowledge (Yu et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022). Research on the reader component includes Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD) (Izacard & Grave, 2022), which employs both DPR and BM25 for retrieval and processes passages independently in the encoder and RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023), which utilizes cross-chunked attention and chunkwise retrieval to generate text grounded on retrieved context.

Text: The tree traversal method first selects the top-k most relevant root nodes based on their cosine similarity to the query embedding. The children of these selected nodes are considered at the next layer and the top- $\mathbf { \nabla } \cdot \mathbf { k }$ nodes are selected from this pool again based on their cosine similarity to the query vector. This process is repeated until we reach the leaf nodes. Finally, the text from all selected nodes is concatenated to form the retrieved context. The algorithm’s steps are outlined below:
Text (level 1): Algorithm 2 Collapsed Tree Algorithm

Text: Querying In this section, we elaborate on the two querying mechanisms employed by RAPTOR: tree traversal and collapsed tree. These methods offer unique ways of traversing the multi-layered RAPTOR tree to retrieve relevant information, each with its own advantages and trade-offs. We provide the pseudocode of both methods in Appendix F. Note that we embed all nodes using SBERT.

Text: The collapsed tree approach offers a simpler way to search for relevant information by considering all nodes in the tree simultaneously, as depicted in Figure 2. Instead of going layer-by-layer, this method flattens the multi-layered tree into a single layer, essentially bringing all the nodes onto the same level for comparison. The steps for this method are outlined below:
Text (level 1): I.2 WHICH LAYERS DO RETRIEVED NODES COME FROM ?

Text: Stephen Robertson, Hugo Zaragoza, et al. The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Beyond. Foundations and Trends in Information Retrieval, 3(4):333–389, 2009. URL https: //doi.org/10.1561/1500000019.

Text: Qualitative Study We conduct a qualitative analysis to understand the benefits of RAP-TOR’s retrieval process compared to Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) methods. Our study focuses on thematic, multi-hop questions using a 1500-word Cinderella fairytale. As illustrated

Text: Devendra Singh Sachan, Mike Lewis, Dani Yogatama, Luke Zettlemoyer, Joelle Pineau, and Manzil Zaheer. Questions are all you need to train a dense passage retriever. Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics, 11:600–616, 2023. doi: 10.1162/tacl a 00564. URL https://aclanthology.org/2023.tacl-1.35.

Based on the provided text, the clustering algorithm in the RAPTOR paper is **based on Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)**, and the **high dimensionality of vector embeddings** presents a key challenge to this approach.

### Key Evidence:
1.  **Base Model:**
    *   Directly stated: *"Our clustering algorithm is based on Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), an approach that offers both flexibility and a probabilistic framework."*
    *   Further elaborated: *"Clustering Algorithm... we employ a clustering algorithm"* (referring to GMMs) and *"After clustering the nodes using Gaussian Mixture Models..."*.

2.  **Challenge:**
    *   Explicitly identified: *"The high dimensionality of vector embeddings presents a challenge for traditional GMMs, as distance metrics may behave poorly when used to measure similarity in high-dimensional spaces (Aggarwal et al., 2001)."*
    *   **Why it's a challenge for GMMs:** GMMs fundamentally rely on distance metrics (like Euclidean distance) to model the underlying Gaussian distributions and assign data points to clusters. In high-dimensional spaces (common for embeddings), these distance metrics become unreliable due to the "curse of dimensionality" – distances between points become less meaningful and tend to converge, making it difficult for GMMs to distinguish meaningful clusters.

### How RAPTOR Addresses the Challenge:
The paper states they mitigate this challenge by:
*   **Employing UMAP:** *"To mitigate this, we employ Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), a manifold learning technique for dimensionality reduction..."*
*   **Creating Hierarchical Structure:** *"The number of nearest neighbors parameter, n neighbors, in UMAP determines the balance between the preservation of local and global structures. Our algorithm varies n neighbors to create a hierarchical clustering structure: it first identifies global clusters and then performs local clustering within these global clusters."*

### Clarification on Other Aspects:
*   **Soft Clustering:** While the paper mentions *"the use of soft clustering, where nodes can belong to multiple clusters without requiring a fixed number of clusters"* as a *feature* of their approach, this is **not** the primary challenge *to the GMM model itself*. Soft clustering is a *characteristic* or *enhancement* they apply *using* GMMs (which inherently support soft assignment). The core challenge *to the GMM model* is the high-dimensionality problem.
*   **Token Threshold:** The recursive clustering when context exceeds token limits is a *consequence* of the summarization step and the need for hierarchical processing, not a direct challenge *to the GMM model*.

**In summary:** The clustering algorithm is fundamentally based on **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)**. The primary challenge to this model in the context of RAPTOR is the **high dimensionality of the vector embeddings**, which causes distance metrics to fail, making traditional GMMs less effective. RAPTOR addresses this by using **UMAP for dimensionality reduction** and **hierarchical clustering** (varying `n_neighbors` in UMAP) to create a multi-level structure.

retrieves.content: Text: Our clustering algorithm is based on Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs), an approach that offers both flexibility and a probabilistic framework. GMMs assume that data points are generated from a mixture of several Gaussian distributions.
Text (level 1): B ABLATION STUDY ON CLUSTERING MECHANISM IN RAPTOR

Text: Clustering Algorithm Clustering plays a key role in building the RAPTOR tree, organizing text segments into cohesive groups. This step groups related content together, which helps the subsequent retrieval process.

Text: One of the unique aspects of our clustering approach is the use of soft clustering, where nodes can belong to multiple clusters without requiring a fixed number of clusters. This flexibility is essential because individual text segments often contain information relevant to various topics, thereby warranting their inclusion in multiple summaries.

Text: Should a local cluster’s combined context ever exceed the summarization model’s token threshold, our algorithm recursively applies clustering within the cluster, ensuring that the context remains within the token threshold.

Text: Model-Based Summarization After clustering the nodes using Gaussian Mixture Models, the nodes in each cluster are sent to a language model for summarization. This step allows the model to transform large chunks of text into concise, coherent summaries of the selected nodes. For our experiments, we use gpt-3.5-turbo to generate the summaries. The summarization step condenses the potentially large volume of retrieved information into a manageable size. We provide statistics on the compression due to the summarization in Appendix C and the prompt used for summarization in Appendix D.
Text (level 1): Algorithm 2 Collapsed Tree Algorithm

Text: The high dimensionality of vector embeddings presents a challenge for traditional GMMs, as distance metrics may behave poorly when used to measure similarity in high-dimensional spaces (Aggarwal et al., 2001). To mitigate this, we employ Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), a manifold learning technique for dimensionality reduction (McInnes et al., 2018). The number of nearest neighbors parameter, n neighbors, in UMAP determines the balance between the preservation of local and global structures. Our algorithm varies n neighbors to create a hierarchical clustering structure: it first identifies global clusters and then performs local clustering within these global clusters. This two-step clustering process captures a broad spectrum of relationships among the text data, from broad themes to specific details.

Text: To group similar text chunks, we employ a clustering algorithm. Once clustered, a Language Model is used to summarize the grouped texts. These summarized texts are then re-embedded, and the cycle of embedding, clustering, and summarization continues until further clustering becomes infeasible, resulting in a structured, multi-layered tree representation of the original documents. An important aspect of RAPTOR is its computational efficiency. The system scales linearly in terms of both build time and token expenditure, making it suitable for processing large and complex corpora. For a comprehensive discussion on RAPTOR’s scalability, please refer to the Appendix A.

Text: In this paper, we have presented RAPTOR, a novel tree-based retrieval system that augments the parametric knowledge of large language models with contextual information at various levels of abstraction. By employing recursive clustering and summarization techniques, RAPTOR creates a hierarchical tree structure that is capable of synthesizing information across various sections of the retrieval corpora. During the query phase, RAPTOR leverages this tree structure for more effective retrieval. Our controlled experiments demonstrated that RAPTOR not only outperforms traditional retrieval methods but also sets new performance benchmarks on several question-answering tasks.

Table: images/2dc9d18c299a370aa5f81efba7513a769cc2c08eb553bdb7afdd2fd7c00516bd.jpg | Body:
Algorithm 1 Tree Traversal Algorithm
function TRAVERSETREE(tree, query, k)
Scurrent tree.layer[0]
for layer in range(tree.num_layers) do topk ←
for node in Scurrent do
score ← dot_product(query, node) top_k.append(node, score))
end for
Slayer ← sorted(top_k)[:k].nodes
Scurrent Slayer end for

Text: Querying In this section, we elaborate on the two querying mechanisms employed by RAPTOR: tree traversal and collapsed tree. These methods offer unique ways of traversing the multi-layered RAPTOR tree to retrieve relevant information, each with its own advantages and trade-offs. We provide the pseudocode of both methods in Appendix F. Note that we embed all nodes using SBERT.
Text (level 1): F PSEUDOCODE FOR RETRIEVAL METHODS

Text: The tree traversal method first selects the top-k most relevant root nodes based on their cosine similarity to the query embedding. The children of these selected nodes are considered at the next layer and the top- $\mathbf { \nabla } \cdot \mathbf { k }$ nodes are selected from this pool again based on their cosine similarity to the query vector. This process is repeated until we reach the leaf nodes. Finally, the text from all selected nodes is concatenated to form the retrieved context. The algorithm’s steps are outlined below:

Table: images/6c292cf099a746649547f2e91ad31ccbac5e14f968824af158d9774eb9a21d97.jpg | Body:
Algorithm 2 Collapsed Tree Algorithm
function COLLAPSEDTREE(tree, query, k, max_tokens) tree flatten(tree) Flatten tree into 1D
top_nodes ←
for node in tree do
top_nodes.append((node, dot_product(query, node))
end for top-nodes ← sorted(topnodes)
result ← ]
total_tokens ← 0 for node in top_nodes do
if total_tokens + node.token_size < max_tokens then
result.append(node)
end if
total_tokens ← total_tokens + node.token_size
end for
return result
end function

Text: The collapsed tree approach offers a simpler way to search for relevant information by considering all nodes in the tree simultaneously, as depicted in Figure 2. Instead of going layer-by-layer, this method flattens the multi-layered tree into a single layer, essentially bringing all the nodes onto the same level for comparison. The steps for this method are outlined below:
Text (level 1): Algorithm 2 Collapsed Tree Algorithm

Text: For querying within this tree, we introduce two distinct strategies: tree traversal and collapsed tree. The tree traversal method traverses the tree layer-by-layer, pruning and selecting the most relevant nodes at each level. The collapsed tree method evaluates nodes collectively across all layers to find the most relevant ones.

Text: By adjusting the depth $d$ and the number of nodes $k$ selected at each layer, the tree traversal method offers control over the specificity and breadth of the information retrieved. The algorithm starts with a broad outlook by considering the top layers of the tree and progressively focuses on finer details as it descends through the lower layers.

retrieves.content: Text: In text, retrieval granularity ranges from fine to coarse, including Token, Phrase, Sentence, Proposition, Chunks, Document. Among them, DenseX [30]proposed the concept of using propositions as retrieval units. Propositions are defined as atomic expressions in the text, each encapsulating a unique factual segment and presented in a concise, self-contained natural language format. This approach aims to enhance retrieval precision and relevance. On the Knowledge Graph (KG), retrieval granularity includes Entity, Triplet, and sub-Graph. The granularity of retrieval can also be adapted to downstream tasks, such as retrieving Item IDs [40]in recommendation tasks and Sentence pairs [38]. Detailed information is illustrated in Table I.

Text: 2) Retrieval Granularity: Another important factor besides the data format of the retrieval source is the granularity of the retrieved data. Coarse-grained retrieval units theoretically can provide more relevant information for the problem, but they may also contain redundant content, which could distract the retriever and language models in downstream tasks [50], [87]. On the other hand, fine-grained retrieval unit granularity increases the burden of retrieval and does not guarantee semantic integrity and meeting the required knowledge. Choosing the appropriate retrieval granularity during inference can be a simple and effective strategy to improve the retrieval and downstream task performance of dense retrievers.

Text (level 1): Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey

Text: In the context of RAG, it is crucial to efficiently retrieve relevant documents from the data source. There are several key issues involved, such as the retrieval source, retrieval granularity, pre-processing of the retrieval, and selection of the corresponding embedding model.
Text (level 1): B. Recursive Retrieval
Text (level 1): A. Iterative Retrieval

Text: Recursive retrieval is often used in information retrieval and NLP to improve the depth and relevance of search results. The process involves iteratively refining search queries based on the results obtained from previous searches. Recursive Retrieval aims to enhance the search experience by gradually converging on the most pertinent information through a feedback loop. IRCoT [61] uses chain-of-thought to guide the retrieval process and refines the CoT with the obtained retrieval results. ToC [57] creates a clarification tree that systematically optimizes the ambiguous parts in the Query. It can be particularly useful in complex search scenarios where the user’s needs are not entirely clear from the outset or where the information sought is highly specialized or nuanced. The recursive nature of the process allows for continuous learning and adaptation to the user’s requirements, often resulting in improved satisfaction with the search outcomes.

Text: In text, retrieval granularity ranges from fine to coarse, including Token, Phrase, Sentence, Proposition, Chunks, Document. Among them, DenseX [30]proposed the concept of using propositions as retrieval units. Propositions are defined as atomic expressions in the text, each encapsulating a unique factual segment and presented in a concise, self-contained natural language format. This approach aims to enhance retrieval precision and relevance. On the Knowledge Graph (KG), retrieval granularity includes Entity, Triplet, and sub-Graph. The granularity of retrieval can also be adapted to downstream tasks, such as retrieving Item IDs [40]in recommendation tasks and Sentence pairs [38]. Detailed information is illustrated in Table I.
